coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanomaterials are indispensable due to their unique properties of high electrical conductivity, mechanical strength and thermal stability, which makes them important nanomaterials in biomedical applications and waste management. Limitations of conventional nanomaterials, such as limited surface area, difficulty in fine tuning electrical or thermal properties and poor dispersibility, calls for the development of advanced nanomaterials to overcome such limitations. Commonly, carbon nanomaterials were synthesized by chemical vapor deposition (CVD), laser ablation or arc discharge methods. The advancement in these techniques yielded monodispersed carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and allows p-type and n-type doping to enhance its electrical and catalytic activities. The functionalized CNTs showed exceptional mechanical, electrical and thermal conductivity (3500–5000 W/mK) properties. On the other hand, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) exhibit strong photoluminescence properties with high quantum yield. Carbon nanohorns are another fascinating type of nanomaterial that exhibit a unique structure with high surface area and excellent adsorption properties. These carbon nanomaterials could improve waste management by adsorbing pollutants from water and soil, enabling precise environmental monitoring, while enhancing wastewater treatment and drug delivery systems. Herein, we have discussed the potentials of all these carbon nanomaterials in the context of innovative waste management solutions, fostering cleaner environments and healthier ecosystems for diverse biomedical applications such as biosensing, drug delivery, and environmental monitoring.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanomaterials are indispensable due to their unique properties of high electrical conductivity, mechanical strength and thermal stability, which makes them important nanomaterials in biomedical applications and waste management. Limitations of conventional nanomaterials, such as limited surface area, difficulty in fine tuning electrical or thermal properties and poor dispersibility, calls for the development of advanced nanomaterials to overcome such limitations. Commonly, carbon nanomaterials were synthesized by chemical vapor deposition (CVD), laser ablation or arc discharge methods. The advancement in these techniques yielded monodispersed carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and allows p-type and n-type doping to enhance its electrical and catalytic activities. The functionalized CNTs showed exceptional mechanical, electrical and thermal conductivity (3500–5000 W/mK) properties. On the other hand, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) exhibit strong photoluminescence properties with high quantum yield. Carbon nanohorns are another fascinating type of nanomaterial that exhibit a unique structure with high surface area and excellent adsorption properties. These carbon nanomaterials could improve waste management by adsorbing pollutants from water and soil, enabling precise environmental monitoring, while enhancing wastewater treatment and drug delivery systems. Herein, we have discussed the potentials of all these carbon nanomaterials in the context of innovative waste management solutions, fostering cleaner environments and healthier ecosystems for diverse biomedical applications such as biosensing, drug delivery, and environmental monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials are in the dimension of 1–100 nm and exhibit unique physical, chemical and biological properties compared to their bulk materials. Thus, nanomaterials can be engineered and manipulated for desired properties that are of scientific and technological relevance [1]. The properties of nanomaterials are influenced by various parameters such as a particle’s size, shape, surface area, structure and composition [2]. Nanomaterials can be engineered into metals, semiconductors, ceramics, carbon-based materials and polymers with large surface areas, prominent quantum effects, size-dependent optical effects, as well as mechanical and thermal effects [3]. Nanomaterials with high surface areas enhance the reactivity of materials and help improve catalytic activities, sensing capabilities, and efficient energy conversion [4].

Conventional and advanced varieties represent different generations of nanomaterials with distinct properties and applications. For example, conventional liposomes are spherical vesicles made of lipid bilayers [5]. They are primarily used for drug delivery because of their ability to encapsulate both hydrophilic and hydrophobic drugs. Polymeric nanoparticles are either composed of synthetic or natural polymers [6], and their conventional varieties are also used for drug delivery, imaging, and diagnostic purposes. Similarly, conventional dendrimers (synthetic macromolecules) are highly branched and used for drug delivery, gene therapy, and imaging applications owing to their well-defined structure and size [7]. Metal oxides, such as iron oxide nanoparticles, are used as contrast agents in magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) and titanium dioxide nanoparticles are employed in sunscreen formulations [8]. Conventional micelles are self-assembled structures formed by surfactant molecules in aqueous solutions, which are used for drug delivery, especially hydrophobic drugs [9]. Nanosuspensions made of submicron-sized solid drug particles dispersed in a liquid medium are used for improving drug solubility and bioavailability. Conventional inorganic nanoparticles, such as gold nanoparticles, are used in diagnostics; and quantum dots are used in imaging and display technologies [10]. It is clear that advanced nanomaterials can be incorporated with multifunctionality for precise engineering [11], such as for targeted drug delivery, tissue engineering, and quantum computing. These materials are often prepared by novel synthesis methods with improved stability, and enhanced properties compared to their conventional counterparts [12].

Recently, carbon nanomaterials, such as carbon quantum dots (CQDs), carbon hollow spheres, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), and carbon nanohorns (CNHs) have found various applications in biomedical fields. They show promise in drug delivery, diagnostics, and tissue engineering, owing to their biocompatibility and tailored functionalities [13]. CNT-based drug delivery systems have enabled targeted and controlled release of therapeutics, minimizing side effects. Additionally, carbon nanomaterials have served as diagnostic tools, facilitating early disease detection through imaging and sensing measurements [14]. Moreover, in sustainable waste management processes, these materials play a vital role. CNTs are used in wastewater treatment and recycling, while various carbon nanomaterials aid in waste valorization and remediation processes [15]. Overall, carbon nanomaterials offer multifaceted solutions across biomedical, environmental, and waste management domains, contributing to advancements in healthcare, sustainability, and environmental protection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of various types of carbon nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanomaterials are known for their exceptional properties, including their high strength, electrical conductivity, and unique chemical reactivity. These materials have garnered significant attention in various fields owing to their potential for revolutionary applications. For example, carbon nanomaterials are used to develop electrochemical biosensors for detecting glucose by enzymatic and non-enzymatic methods [16]. CNTs are typically produced by chemical vapor deposition (CVD), laser ablation (see Fig. 1) or arc-discharge methods (see Fig. 2). CVD mainly involves the catalytic growth of nanotubes from carbon-containing gases. The thermal CVD method is used to synthesize boron nitride (BN) and boron carbon nitride (BCN) nanomaterials, as well as for developing electrochemical or optical assays of pollutants and as a drug carrier for drug delivery [17]. Generally, CNTs are synthesized as single-walled (SWCNTs) and multi-walled (MWCNTs) carbon nanotubes with unique properties for various applications. For instance, the Al-CNT composite is prepared by using plasma sintering or ball milling, which improves the corrosion resistance of the composites [18]. The SWCNTs can be metallic or have semiconducting properties based on their structure [19]. Few essential parameters such as temperature and pressure optimization are necessary during the synthesis of CNTs. To enhance the process, CNT is computationally designed from acetylene for CVD in a wafer scale-up process [20].

Graphene can be obtained by mechanical exfoliation (e.g., the Scotch tape method), chemical reduction of graphene oxide, or CVD on metallic substrates [21]. Few-layered graphene and monolayer graphene are common forms, with the latter being a single layer of carbon atoms arranged in a hexagonal lattice. The doping on graphene monolayer enhances the electrocatalytic process in oxygen-reduction reactions (ORR). For example, synthesis of N-doped graphene is reported by an eco-friendly approach [22]. Fullerenes, such as C60, are typically synthesized by vaporizing graphite in an inert atmosphere. They can also be produced in soot from combustion processes. The most well-known fullerene is C60, which forms a spherical structure, but other fullerene shapes also exist. Modified fullerenes with NiVCuO is used for dye degradation. Carbon dots (CDs) are typically derived from carbon-rich precursors through processes like hydrothermal synthesis or laser ablation [23,24]. CDs are ultra-small carbon nanoparticles of less than 10 nm and can exhibit quantum confinement effects. CD-based heterogeneous catalysts are used for photocatalytic applications [25]. Carbon nanohorns are typically produced by laser ablation or chemical vapor deposition. They have a horn-shaped structure with exceptional adsorption properties in two different forms, such as single-walled carbon nanohorns (SWCNHs) and multi-walled carbon nanohorns (MWCNHs). Carbon nanohorns are used to enhance oxygen evolution reactions (OERs) under high current densities. Nickel-filled core/shell carbon nanohorns fulfill the requirement of a catalyst [26]. Carbon nanofibers (CNFs) are synthesized through various techniques like electrospinning, CVD or catalytic growth. CNFs are produced in various forms, including in straight, helical, or branched structures. The ZnO/CNFs have found potential application in tissue engineering and do not exhibit any adverse effects on hypotriploid human cell lines [27]. Each type of carbon nanomaterial offers unique properties and applications, ranging from nanoelectronics to drug delivery systems, reinforcing materials, and energy storage devices. The choice of fabrication methods and materials depend on the desired properties and their intended applications [28].

Each one of the fabrication methods show potential advantages during the synthesis of carbon nanomaterials. For example, the CVD method is used to obtain high quality carbon nanomaterials such as CNT with precise control on composition, morphology and properties for surface coating applications [31]. CNTs are synthesized by the CVD method under high temperatures (600–900℃) using methane on a catalytic substrate (e.g., Fe, Co or Ni), which forms a cylindrical nanotube with enhanced electrical, thermal and mechanical properties. For graphene synthesis, very high temperatures of around 800–1000℃ have to be applied with the precursor gas on catalysts [32]. Copper catalysts are employed for the synthesis of graphene [33]. For the laser ablation synthesis of CNTs and graphene, a high-powered laser beam irradiates a carbon target (such as graphite) in the presence of a reactive gas (like helium or hydrogen), causing the target material to vaporize and form a plasma, from which CNTs and graphene are produced through condensation and nucleation processes, offering a rapid and versatile method for the fabrication of nanostructured carbon materials with precise control over the sizes, structures, and purity of the nanomaterials [34].

In the electrochemical synthesis of graphene, a carbon source, typically graphite, is subjected to an electric current in an electrolyte solution, leading to the exfoliation of graphene flakes [35]. Similarly, the laser ablation method is used to grow CNTs on electrode surfaces, offering a facile and scalable approach for producing carbon-based nanostructures with potential applications in sensors, and catalysis [34] (see Fig. 3).

All carbon nanomaterials, including CNTs, graphene, graphene oxide, carbon quantum dots, and nano horns, share key characteristics [37] such as exceptional mechanical strength, high electrical conductivity, and a large surface area-to-volume ratio. Their unique structures offer diverse applications in electronics, energy storage, and biomedical fields. CNTs are cylindrical carbon structures with extraordinary strength and conductivity [38]. Graphene, a single layer of carbon atoms arranged in a hexagonal lattice, exhibits outstanding electrical and thermal conductivity. Graphene oxide features oxygen-containing functional groups, enhancing its solubility and compatibility [39]. Carbon quantum dots exhibit quantum confinement effects with tunable optical properties. Carbon nano horns offer unique chemical and mechanical properties for sensor and catalytic applications [40].

Carbon nanospheres (CNSs) are spherical carbon nanoparticles with a wide range of applications in materials science, nanotechnology, and medicine. They come in various types and can be fabricated through a variety of methods [41]. Fullerenes are perhaps the most well-known types of CNSs. They are hollow, cage-like structures made up of carbon atoms arranged in a soccer ball-like pattern. Fullerene-based sandwiches have controllable thermal properties and charge transfer possibility in thermo-electronics [42]. The most famous fullerene is C60, but there are many other fullerene derivatives with distinct properties. They are typically produced through techniques like arc discharge, laser ablation, and chemical vapor deposition. For instance, polyamino-fullerenes are used as CO2 scavengers for free energy capturing in energetics [43]. The arc discharge method is a prominent technique for synthesizing fullerenes. In this process, two graphite electrodes are placed in a high-purity inert gas atmosphere (typically helium) inside a reaction chamber. Studies show that the microwave and arc-discharge methods are the most convenient methods to produce fullerenes for industrial applications [44]. A high electric current is passed through electrodes, creating an intense arc. This arc vaporizes the graphite, causing carbon atoms to cluster into closed-cage structures, primarily fullerene molecules. A helical graphite whiskers are also produced as byproducts of fullerenes at high temperatures of around 2600°C [45]. The vaporized carbon then condenses on to a cooler surface in the chamber, forming soot rich in fullerenes. This soot is subsequently extracted and purified to isolate various fullerene species, including the well-known C60 and C70 variants. The arc discharge method remains crucial for fullerene production due to its reliability and versatility. Fullerene derivatives are also synthesized using indene and thiophene by experimental and theoretical approaches [46]. The laser ablation method is a versatile technique for synthesizing fullerenes. In this process, a high-energy laser beam is focused on to a target material, typically a pure graphite rod or a carbon-containing solid, in a controlled atmosphere, often helium or argon gas. For instance, fullerene like MoS2 has been synthesized in water. This method is for the synthesis of biocompatible nanomaterials [47]. The intense laser energy rapidly heats and vaporizes the target, causing carbon atoms to form clusters and, subsequently, condenses into closed-cage fullerene structures, such as C60 and C70, as they cool and expand in the gas phase. The biocompatibility of fullerenes have been increased by coating with dextran, which shows remarkable effects on fibroblast cells [48]. The resulting mixture of fullerenes is collected on a suitable substrate or trapped in a cold surface, followed by purification steps to isolate the desired fullerene species. Laser ablation offers precise control and the potential for generating diverse fullerene variants [49]. CVD is a method for synthesizing fullerenes. In this process, a carbon-containing precursor gas, often a hydrocarbon like methane, is introduced into a high-temperature reactor chamber. Inside the chamber, the precursor gas decomposes when exposed to extreme heat, releasing carbon atoms [49]. These carbon atoms then reassemble into fullerene structures as they cool and accumulate on a suitable substrate. The choice of precursor gas and specific deposition conditions can influence the size and composition of the final fullerenes [50]. CVD offers precise control over fullerene growth and purity, making it a valuable method for tailored fullerene production, including for large-scale manufacturing and research purposes. However, your choice of production method depends on your specific requirements. The biocompatibility of C70 fullerene is tested with the interaction of threonine, which results in a drug carrier for transporting human serum albumin. It shows a noticeable antioxidant property when it reacts with 2,2-diphenylpicrylhydrazyl (DPPH) [51]. If you prioritize high purity and relatively high yield of C60 and C70, the arc discharge method might be a preferred choice. Laser ablation offers good purity and versatility in producing various fullerene species. The influence of temperature causes variations in the concentration of fullerenes (eg., C60 and C70) in different regions in the arc discharge chamber. They exhibited additional absorption peaks at 285 and 305 nm [52]. CVD can be tailored for specific applications and may require additional steps to achieve high purity. Researchers often choose the method that best aligns with their desired fullerene characteristics and quantities. Moreover, carbon nanomaterials such as CNTs, fullerenes and nano diamonds find application as efficient stationary phase material in chromatographic techniques [53].

Carbon nanoparticles are solid spheres made of carbon atoms. They can vary in size from a few nanometers to several micrometers, and are often produced through chemical methods such as pyrolysis, hydrothermal synthesis, or combustion processes [54,55]. Other methods for synthesizing carbon nanoparticles include CVD, laser ablation, and hydrothermal synthesis [56]. Pyrolysis methods allow precise control over the size and morphology of carbon nanoparticles by adjusting reaction parameters like temperature and precursor concentrations [57]. These methods can yield highly pure carbon nanoparticles as they often involve the decomposition of a single carbon-containing precursor [58]. Some pyrolysis techniques, such as CVD, can be scaled up for industrial production, making them suitable for large-scale manufacturing [59]. The disadvantages of many pyrolysis methods include the requirement of high temperature and energy inputs, leading to high production costs. Moreover, carbon nanoparticles produced through pyrolysis may have limited surface functionalization, making it challenging to tailor their properties for specific applications [60]. Some pyrolysis methods, like CVD and laser ablation, involve complex equipment, which can be expensive to acquire and maintain [61]. High-temperature pyrolysis processes can also generate greenhouse gases (GHG) and other pollutants if not properly controlled, posing environmental challenges [62].

CVD is a versatile method for synthesizing carbon nanoparticles. In this method, a carbon-containing precursor gas, such as methane, is introduced into a reactor chamber at elevated temperatures [63]. The precursor gas decomposes, releasing carbon atoms that subsequently reassemble on a substrate to form carbon nanoparticles. The size, structure, and morphology of the nanoparticles can be precisely controlled by adjusting various factors such as temperature, gas flow rates, and substrate properties. CVD is also used for fabricating semiconductor devices to increase high electron mobility transistors (HEMT) power in electronics [64]. CVD offers several advantages, including high purity, scalability for industrial production, and the ability to produce tailored nanoparticles with unique properties. It has been widely used in applications like nanoelectronics, catalysis, and nanocomposite materials [65]. Laser ablation is also used to fabricate carbon nanoparticles. In this process, a high-energy laser beam is focused on a carbon-containing target, often graphite, in a controlled environment. The intense laser energy vaporizes and dislodges carbon atoms from the target material [66]. These atoms then cool and condense in the surrounding environment, forming carbon nanoparticles. Laser ablation offers precise control over nanoparticle sizes, and can produce a variety of carbon nanomaterials with different properties. Gold-based catalysts are also synthesized by the pulsed laser ablation method, which is used in photo- and electrocatalytic applications [67]. Hydrothermal synthesis is also used for producing carbon nanoparticles under high-temperature and high-pressure, water-based conditions. In this process, a carbon precursor such as sugar solution, carbohydrates, or other carbon sources are placed in a sealed reactor and heated at temperatures above 180°C for a specific duration. For instance, CdO-SnO nano flakes are synthesized by the sol-gel method to develop hybrid supercapacitors [68]. The high-pressure and high-temperature environment promotes carbonization, creating carbon nanoparticles with controlled sizes and morphology. Comparatively, hydrothermal synthesis offers advantages such as simplicity, scalability, and the potential to create functionalized carbon nanoparticles. A one-pot hydrothermal synthesis is used to prepare GO nanoparticles to enhance the antibacterial efficacy of antibiotics and an AI-based approach that helps in environmental remediation [69]. However, it typically requires longer reaction times and may involve post-synthesis purification steps to obtain highly pure carbon nanoparticles [70]. Pyrolysis methods can also offer excellent control over the synthesis of carbon nanoparticles, including precise, controlled size and high purity [68]. However, they can be energy-intensive and may require sophisticated equipment, along with environmental considerations. Finally, the choice of production methods should align with one’s desired nanoparticle characteristics and application requirements [71].

CQDs are small, quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles with sizes typically less than 10 nanometers. They exhibit quantum confinement effects and are commonly used in optoelectronics, bioimaging, and sensors. CQDs are typically synthesized by carbonization of organic precursors or through bottom-up approaches [72]. There are several fabrication methods to synthesize CQDs, each with its own advantages and characteristics. CQDs can be synthesized through hydrothermal or solvothermal methods by heating carbon-containing precursors, such as glucose or citric acid, in water or organic solvents at elevated temperatures. Velasco et al. used CQDs in environmental remediation as a catalyst for detecting and degrading pollutants [73]. Microwave irradiation of carbon precursors in the presence of suitable reactants can rapidly produce CQDs. This method offers shorter reaction times and can yield highly luminescent CQDs. Bressi et al. developed CQDs from a renewable biomass using hydrothermal and microwave approaches for waste management [74]. Laser ablation of carbon-based targets in a liquid environment can produce CQDs. This method offers excellent control over size and surface properties, making it suitable for tailored CQD synthesis. Nair et al. prepared CQDs from various precursors for antiviral activity against Covid-19, using the hydrothermal approach [75]. Carbonaceous materials, like citric acid or carbon tetrachloride, can be pyrolyzed at high temperatures to generate CQDs. This method is relatively simple and cost-effective. CQDs also can be synthesized by electrochemical methods, where carbon precursors are electrolyzed to produce CQDs with controlled properties. This technique offers good control over size and shape [76]. Ultrasonication of carbon precursors can generate CQDs as well. This method is particularly useful for producing small-sized and highly luminescent CQDs. Oxidation of carbon precursors, such as graphite, in the presence of strong oxidizing agents can also lead to CQD formation. This method can yield CQDs with well-defined properties [77]. The choice of method depends on the desired characteristics of the CQDs, including size, optical properties, and surface functionalization. Researchers often select the most appropriate synthesis method based on the specific applications and properties required for their CQD-based materials. Recently, Zhao et al. developed cobalt QDs coated with mineral carbon for adsorbing tetracycline in environmental remediations [78].

Carbon hollow spheres can be fabricated using templates, where a sacrificial core is coated with carbon material and is finally removed, leaving a hollow sphere behind [79]. Alternatively, they can be created through chemical vapor deposition. To fabricate carbon hollow spheres using the hydrothermal method, a carbonaceous precursor, often glucose or sucrose, is dissolved in water to create a homogeneous solution [80]. This solution is sealed in a high-pressure hydrothermal reactor, heated to a specific temperature (typically 150–250°C), which is maintained for a certain period. Under these high-pressure and high-temperature conditions, the carbon precursor undergoes carbonization, forming carbon hollow spheres [81]. After cooling and depressurizing, the resulting carbon hollow spheres are recovered, washed to remove impurities, and then dried. This method allows for precise control over the sizes and properties of the carbon hollow spheres, making them valuable in various applications, including catalysis and drug delivery. Recently, Yang et al. suggested the use of micro-sized nanoaggregates prepared by the spray-drying method, which showed potential advantages in sensors, catalysis and biomedicine [82].

CNHs are unique carbon nanostructures resembling tiny horn-shaped particles with a diameter of a few nanometers. They are typically fabricated through a process called arc discharge or laser ablation. In the arc discharge process, a mixture of graphite and metal catalysts (usually nickel or iron) are vaporized in a high-temperature chamber filled with inert gas [82]. An electric arc is then generated, which triggers carbon atoms to form CNHs from the catalytic action of metal particles. In the laser ablation process, a high-energy laser beam irradiates a carbon target in the presence of a metal catalyst, leading to the vaporization and subsequent formation of CNHs [82]. The choice of catalyst and process conditions can influence the size and morphology of CNHs. After synthesis, CNHs are purified and catalyst residues are removed [83,84]. CNHs find diverse applications in drug delivery, energy storage, and catalyst support, owing to their high surface area and unique nanostructure. Single walled CNHs offer a high surface area, a unique conical structure, good electrical conductivity, and potential for drug delivery and gas storage applications [85,86]. They can serve as catalyst supports and have reactive edges. However, CNH production is a complex and expensive process, often resulting in impurities and defects [87]. Their biocompatibility and toxicity require careful consideration. Commercial applications of CNHs are more limited than other carbon nanomaterials, and dispersion issues can hinder their use [88]. Nevertheless, ongoing research may address these challenges and expand CNH applications across diverse fields.

The role of nanomaterials in the fields of drug and gene delivery, tissue engineering, target-specific treatment methods, etc., is now widely accepted. Carbon-based nanomaterials have shown certain advantages over other available nanomaterials. Their main advantage (e.g., CNTs, CDs, graphene-based nanomaterials, carbon nanofibres, etc.) is their biocompatibility [89]. Depending on their structure, hybridization, size, and morphology, different carbon nanomaterials exhibit different optical, electrical, and physical characteristics, which in turn dictate their interaction with drug molecules, loading capacities, and sustainability inside a physiological medium, etc. [90]. In this review, we have tried to highlight a wide range of biomedical applications of various carbon nanomaterials.

Since their invention, CNTs have been elements of much interest in the field of biomedical application owing to their impressive biocompatibility, high length-to-width ratio, and low electrical resistance, among other factors [91]. CNTs can be of two types, based on their shapes and sizes, viz. (i) single walled (SWCNTs) and (ii) multi-walled (MWCNTs). CNTs have been widely explored in diverse biomedical applications due to their large surface area, leading to high drug loading efficiency. Their inert nature leads to better accumulation inside cells, while their small sizes lead to easy internalization and multi-functionalization abilities [92–94]. Attributed to easy functionalization, CNTs have been used for the selective delivery of peptides, nucleic acids, proteins, and even smaller drug molecules. Another advantage of CNTs over other drug carriers is their ability to absorb the near infrared (NIR) range, hence showing facile penetration deep into diseased tissues with low to minimum side effects [95,96]. An enormous amount of work has been focused on the use of CNTs as drug delivery vehicles, photodynamic therapeutic agents, and photothermal agents, among others [97]. In the present section, we analyze some of the recent applications of CNTs in the field of biomedicine. Drugs or other biologically relevant compounds can be incorporated into CNTs via both covalent and non-covalent modifications. Although noncovalent modification causes minimum alteration to the CNT structure, it might lead to untimely leaching of the drug, causing undesirable side effects. Therefore, decorating the biological cargo covalently on to the CNT leads to a more stable material, which is also beneficial for controlled drug release.

Major applications of CNTs have been observed in the field of cancer therapy. Recently, Gangrade and Mandal reported using a silk fiber-based hydrogel combined with CNT to achieve greater mechanical strength for the delivery vehicle [98]. They had applied these hydrogel systems for the target-specific delivery of anticancer drug, doxorubicin, to folic acid receptor positive cells (see Fig. 4). The authors monitored the process and saw that the release of doxorubicin could be dictated by the low pH of the cancer cells. Low pH and elevated temperatures jointly contributed to the controlled release of doxorubicin owing to the degradation of the hydrogel structure under these conditions.

In another example, Li et al. produced an implant by incorporating CNTs on to a mesoporous silica matrix. This implant was utilized for delivering a representative anticancer drug, doxorubicin; while the heat generation ability of the CNTs was also explored for photothermal treatment against cancer [99]. In another recent example by Khan et. al, MWCNTs were conjugated with hydroxyapatite nanorods grafted on to a polypropylene [99,100]. Calculation of Young’s modulus validated the greater mechanical strength due to the incorporation of CNTs. Further, the authors discussed their application in orthopedic treatments owing to the heat generation ability of CNTs. In a very recent work, a detailed theoretical study was presented for the target-specific delivery of a well-known anticancer drug, doxorubicin, using folic acid decorated CNTs [101]. They had observed stronger interaction of doxorubicin with the folic acid decorated CNTs compared to the bare CNTs with carboxylic acid groups on its surface. This interaction could be attributed to the presence of negative charges on the surface of CNTs, while doxorubicin had no charge. The authors monitored the Lennard–Jones potentials using the density functional theory (DFT) simulation model for the assessment of the drug release profile from the CNTs. The folate-functionalization and pH-dependent drug release mechanism improves the release of doxorubicin from CNTs near the cancer cells. The adsorption simulation validated the change in the folate receptor structure on the cancer cell membrane upon interaction with the folic acid decorated CNTs [93]. Such change in morphology helped in easy internalization and release of doxorubicin. There have been several other theoretical studies and quantum mechanical studies in recent times to confirm the drug delivery potentials of CNTs.

Graphene is one of the most important allotropes of carbon. The intriguing hexagonal two-dimensional structure of graphene derivatives endow them with diverse optoelectronic, physicochemical, and biomedical activities [102]. This two-dimensional, honeycomb-like layered structure of sp2-hybridised carbon atoms show strong absorption properties in the longer visible or infrared wavelength region. Such extended absorption makes graphene-based materials promising tools for various biomedical applications [103]. Further, the porosity, broad surface area, and easy functionalization make graphene-based materials handy tools for drug delivery. There are several graphene-based materials (e.g., graphene oxide, reduced graphene oxide, graphene quantum dots, graphene nanoribbons, etc.) that are currently being tested for use in biomedicine [104]. In the following section, we summarize some of this recent research on the application of graphene-based materials in the area of biomedicine.

Sarkar et al. showcased the use of hyaluronic acid functionalized graphene oxide quantum dots for target-specific treatment of type II diabetes mellitus [105] (see Fig. 5). Type II diabetes is greatly related to obesity of the patient. According to several recent findings, liver inflammation is closely related to both obesity and diabetes. Hence, in this present study, authors focused on developing target specific graphene oxide quantum dots decorated with hyaluronic acid on its surface as hyaluronic acid can serve as a potent biocompatible ligand to the abundant CD44 markers on the membranes of inflamed cells. The graphene quantum dots were further loaded with antidiabetic and anti-inflammatory drug metformin. Thus-developed graphene oxide quantum dots were efficiently internalized into the inflamed RAW264.7 cells and showed excellent dual effect of anti-inflammation and anti-oxidant (Fig. 5). In another recent work, authors developed a nanohydrogel by combining chitosan, agarose gel, and graphene oxide nanoparticles for the treatment of breast cancers [106]. They functionalized the graphene oxide nanoparticles with pH-sensitive biocompatible polysaccharide chitosan to achieve target-specificity. The authors obtained the hydrogel upon treating chitosan with agarose gel under room temperature stirring. Elsayed et al. used a hydrogel for successful delivery of the anticancer drug, 5-fluorouracil [107]. Almost 92 % of the drug was loaded into the hydrogel via crosslinking in the presence of glyoxal. The high drug loading efficiency can be attributed to the strong hydrogen bonding between the amide linkages of the hydrogel with the electronegative oxygen or fluorine atoms present in the drug molecule [108]. Under the acidic condition (pH 5.4) inside the breast cancer cell line, the chitosan polymer structure of chitosan collapses owing to the protonation of the free amine groups present here. Therefore, almost 65 % of the anticancer drug, 5-fluorouracil was selectively released inside the breast cancer cells after 24 hours of treatment [109]. This study showcases a stimuli-responsive, target-specific, graphene oxide nanoparticle-based hydrogel for effective treatment of breast cancer cells [110].

Besides anticancer treatment, recently graphene oxide quantum dots have also been explored for other diseases like age-related macular degeneration, wound healing, antimicrobial treatment, etc. In a recent work, Huang et al. discuss about the blindness caused by Choroidal Neovascularization in elderly people [111]. In their work, they explore the anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties of graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs), as discussed earlier. GOQDs have been found to restrict the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), which in turn reduce the oxidative stress and inflammation inside the cell [112]. According to their study, they functionalized the GOQDs with minocycline via the usual EDC coupling reaction. They introduced Minocycline, an efficient anti-inflammatory drug to selectively target the overexpression of matrix metalloproteinase 9 in the inflamed cells [113]. Further, the GOQDs were functionalized with hydrophobic 18-carbon polymer C18 for effective interaction with the retinal macrophages. These C18-decorated, minocycline-loaded GOQDs also exhibited controlled angiogenesis effects [114].

There are several other graphene-based nanomaterials with excellent utility in the biomedical field, such as graphene nanoribbons and reduced graphene oxides [115]. The main physical property of reduced graphene oxides lies in their efficient conversion of light energy to heat energy [116]. Thus, studies have explored the use of reduced graphene oxides in cancer treatments like photothermal therapy (PTT) and photodynamic therapy (PDT) [117,118]. However, owing to the absence of functional groups in the reduced graphene oxide structure, they are less significant for drug delivery than other graphene materials. When the hexagonal layers are arranged in strips with a width about 50 nm, the graphene nanomaterials are known as nanoribbons [119]. The intriguing property of nanoribbons is their prominent interaction with DNA nucleobases. Nelson et al. devised a DNA sensor using the in-plane current generation within the pores of graphene nanoribbons [120]. However, even after several successful implementations of drug/gene delivery, PTT, PDT, or anti-inflammatory applications, graphene nanomaterials have suffered from solubility issues in the physiological medium and lack of target specificity [121]. Hence, researchers are still developing graphene-based composites with polymers, magnetic metal nanoparticles, peptides, etc., for better in vitro and in vivo results [122].

CDs are unique zero-dimensional, carbon nanomaterial with sizes of less than 20 nm. These CDs have efficiently replaced the use of heavy metal-based quantum dots as drug delivery vehicles. CDs have fascinating photoluminescence properties, less photobleaching, enhanced biocompatibility and promising hydrophilicity [123]. These carbon nanomaterials can be synthesized by diverse means, such as the hydrothermal method, electrochemical exfoliation, microwave-assisted green synthesis, and simple organic reactions from small molecules [124]. Interestingly, the synthesis method determines the luminescence properties of CDs, depending on their sizes. Further, incorporation of heteroatoms like nitrogen, phosphorous, sulphur, etc., have brought about distinct changes in the fluorescence spectra of CDs [125], which can be attributed to changes in excited-state energy levels, direct recombination, varied vibrational relaxation, etc. Such tunable fluorescence properties of CDs have been monitored in real-time for drug delivery and drug-action in the physiological system [126].

Recently, Devi and Wangoo et al. illustrated nitrogen-doped CDs for pH-triggered delivery of the anticancer drug, doxorubicin [125]. Introduction of the nitrogen atom using excess arginine with uric acid has provided a new, lowest occupied molecular orbital (LUMO) energy level, which has been corroborated by a broad absorption peak in the range of 300–400 nm (see Fig. 6). Intersystem crossing from this new LUMO to oxygen-based, highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) has led to the creation of unique blue fluorescent CDs [127]. The pH of the medium was found to be affecting the fluorescence properties of the CDs to a great extent. This was probably because the strong aggregation among the CDs via hydrogen bonding of amine, hydroxyl, and/or carboxylic acid functional groups led to a drop in fluorescence intensity; however, under basic conditions, deprotonation resulted in restoration of the bright blue fluorescence [128]. This was also corroborated by the decrease in fluorescence intensity upon gradual loading of the positive charge-bearing doxorubicin, which induced aggregation among the CDs. When tested in vitro, the CDs displayed a sharp rise in fluorescence intensity indicating the release of doxorubicin from their surface [129]. Thus, these nitrogen-doped CDs have been successfully used for pH-activated delivery of doxorubicin and real-time surveillance of drug action [130].

In another study, Talib and Mohammad had presented the design and development of biotin-coated CDs for redox-triggered target-specific delivery of the leukemia drug, 6-mercaptopurine [131]. In this work, the authors synthesized the carbon dots by a hydrothermal method and conjugated the drug via a vinyl sulfide linker, which could act as a redox-sensitive, self-immolative linker. Cancer cells generally experience high oxidative stress with abundant glutathione in the cancer cells [132]. Many of the cancer cells are overexpressed with biotin receptors on their surface, hence as-synthesized carbon dots can ensure specific internalization of carbon dots inside the cancer cells alone. Such multi-functionalization is possible only due to the multiple functional groups on the surface of the carbon dots [133]. In another recent work, carbon dot-based lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) were synthesized by Kim et al. for target-specific delivery of anticancer drugs like gemcitabine or doxorubicin [134]. They had developed pH-sensitive carbon dots with multiple functional groups, which in turn were conjugated with fatty acids of varied chain lengths, resulting in the generation of LNPs named coposomes.

These coposomes were found to have a hydrophilic head of carbon dots and a hydrophobic tail formed from the fatty acid chain [134]. To validate the use of these coposomes for a diverse range of biologically important molecules, positively charged methylene blue, negatively charged gemcitabine, and neutral doxorubicin had been used [76]. Gemcitabine showed maximum encapsulation into the coposomes. These drug-encapsulated coposomes exhibited intriguing change in surface charge from negative to positive on moving from neutral to acidic pH level inside the cancer cells, leading to easy internalization through the anionic outer membranes of cancer cells [140]. Further, coposomes showed an initial delay in drug release of about 10–20 % during the first 24 hours, which signified the stability of coposomes as drug delivery vehicles [141].

Very recently, Lu et al. constructed ferrocenylselenodopamine drug-incorporated carbon dots for selective destruction of cancer cells via hydroxyl radical generation [142]. Incorporation of a ferrocene unit rendered these carbon dots with redox-responsiveness with the shuffle in the oxidation states of ferrocene and ferrocene+. Such changes in the oxidation state also resulted in the periodic switching on and off of the fluorescence intensities of carbon dots owing to the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) from the polyethyleneimine (PEI) units of the carbon dots and the ferrocene unit [143]. These carbon dots could efficiently produce hydroxyl radical in a way similar to Fenton’s reagent and bring about promising specific cytotoxicity to the cancerous HeLa cells in response to the reducing environment of the cancerous cells [144].

Besides such promising applications in anticancer treatment, carbon dots have also found use in antibacterial [145], antifungal [146–148], and antiviral applications [149–151]. Considering the plethora of applications of carbon dots in a range of biomedical areas, much focus is given to develop new classes of carbon dot-based biomaterials and other composites [152]. Even different green synthetic approaches are being explored for the sustainable improvement of carbon dot-based drug delivery vehicles [153]. In addition, carbon nanomaterials play a pivotal role in revolutionizing cancer diagnostics and therapy by offering unique advantages in early detection of diagnostic markers, dual drug delivery, smart drug release, and DNA biosensing. These materials enable early cancer diagnosis through highly sensitive imaging modalities like photoacoustic and fluorescent imaging, facilitating the visualization of tumors and metastases at early stages [135]. Additionally, functionalized carbon nanomaterials can enhance the detection of specific cancer biomarkers such as carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) and microRNAs, aiding in disease prognosis and personalized treatment strategies [136]. Moreover, carbon nanomaterials serve as versatile carriers for simultaneous delivery of multiple therapeutic agents, allowing synergistic effects and precise control over drug release kinetics, while enabling on-demand drug release at the tumor site through stimuli-responsive systems [137]. Furthermore, their large surface area and excellent electrical properties make them ideal platforms for DNA biosensors, facilitating the sensitive and selective detection of genetic mutations associated with cancer predisposition [138]. Despite these remarkable benefits, challenges such as biocompatibility concerns, potential toxicity, complex synthesis and functionalization, biodistribution issues, and regulatory hurdles need to be addressed to ensure the safe and effective translation of carbon nanomaterial-based technologies into clinical practice [139]. See Table 1 for a summary of the major advantages and some disadvantages of carbon nanomaterials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of carbon nanomaterials as diagnostic tools
ce:section.ce:para: Early diagnosis for any disease can be extremely helpful in treating patients. There are several instruments and testing methodologies available for the identification of diseases; however, most of these processes are highly advanced, expensive, and time-consuming [154]. In this regard, utilization of optical, electrical, and physicochemical characteristics of certain elements have proved to be beneficial. These elements, which can be used to identify specific biological entities like enzymes, peptides, metal ions, antibodies and amino acids, or other relevant analytes, are often known as biosensors [155]. Moreover, carbon nanomaterials with excellent optical and electrical properties are being constantly explored in the field of optical biosensing, electrochemical biosensing, bioimaging and contrasting agents [156]. In the following sections, we have summarized some recent literature on the biosensing properties of carbon nanomaterials (see Table 2).

By virtue of their extraordinary physicochemical and optical properties, CNTs are used in early diagnosis of various diseases. Their intriguing luminescence properties have enabled them to act as prominent optical sensors [157] (see Fig. 7). Further, owing to their unique width-to-length ratio, CNTs are known to have extraordinary electrical conductivities, which make them handy tools as electrochemical biosensors [158].

One of the most promising diagnosis mechanisms involve monitoring the breath samples of patients. Analyzing such breath samples is a handier, faster, cheaper, and non-invasive mode of diagnosis. Owing to their one-dimensional, porous nature, CNTs are capable of absorbing gaseous substances on their surfaces [159]. This property is now being largely utilized by several research groups for the detection of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) such as ammonia, acetone, nitric oxide, methanol, ethanol, etc. Recently, Shooshtari et al. constructed a TiO2 modified CNT for the detection of VOCs (acetone and ethanol) [160]. In their study, they had deposited CNTs on the TiO2 surface for better electron transportation and had increased the number of reaction sites. The electronic nose was devised by depositing four different electrodes of Ag, Au, Al, and Pt on the surface of a CNT-TiO2 composite and exposing it to the specific VOCs. TiO2 here acted as the sensitive layer and the CNT layer at the bottom was responsible for electrical conductance [161]. In this composite, CNT behaved as the p-type conductor in conjunction with the n-type TiO2. The O-ions on the TiO2 layer formed the electron depletion layer; however, upon interaction with the VOCs, the O- ions released charge carriers into the conduction band of the n-type layer, which in turn was transferred to the CNT layer [162]. This led to a change in the concentration of holes in the p-type CNT layer. Thus synthesized, the CNT-TiO2 composite behaved as a concentration-sensitive electric nose for VOCs [163].

Recently, Li et al. presented another electrical nose for the detection of VOCs related to Covid-19 [164]. Following the line of research for the early diagnosis of influenza through a breath sample analysis, the authors successfully developed a swappable sensor comprising 64 different arrays of carbon nanomaterials including graphene, SWCNTs, and polymer-coated SWCNTs [165]. These electrical biosensors were applied for the detection of VOCs from Covid-19 infected lungs or respiratory systems. This device could efficiently detect Covid-19 breath-markers like H2S, CO, NO, etc., with a detection limit as low as 2–5 ppb [166]. Such detection can be attributed to the large surface area of the CNTs leading to high absorption of gases. There are several important VOCs associated with critical diseases such as cancer and diabetes. Various CNT-based electronic noses have also found wide applications in the early diagnosis of these diseases [167,168].

In addition to the usage of CNTs as electrical biosensors, they have also shown promise as optical sensors in terms of distinct color change or change in fluorescence property for various biologically relevant analytes. In a recent work by Wulf and Bisker, SWCNTs were incorporated into a hydrogel structure formed by self-assembling protected amino acids to monitor the change in morphology of the hydrogel in connection with the intracellular heterogeneous environment [169]. To avoid the use of any large and expensive microscopic set-ups like AFM and SEM to monitor the morphological changes, fluorescence spectroscopy can be a handy and non-invasive technology. SWCNTs are known to exhibit prominent emission in the longer, near-infrared wavelength region, depending on their structural proximity and semiconducting nature [170]. Here, the authors dispersed the SWCNTs into a solution of fluorenylmethyloxycarbonyl-functionalized dipeptide hydrogel of phenylalanine to track the changes in chirality of the hydrogel under physiological conditions. Such encapsulation had also improved the hydrophilicity of SWCNTs [171].

There are several biologically important enzymes, whose malfunctioning can lead to serious abnormalities in our physiological processes. For example, any kind of interruption in the normal working of acetylcholinesterase can cause lethal diseases like Alzheimer’s, Parkinson’s, organophosphorous poisoning, and cancer [172]. Therefore, real-time monitoring of the functions of such enzymes are of utmost importance. Loewenthal et al. constructed a SWCNT-based fluorescent probe for selective detection of cholinesterase and inhibited its activity [173]. They had synthesized a DNA-SWCNT conjugate at room temperature by bath-sonication followed by tip-sonication in an ice bath. The study had observed prominent fluorescence change for (T)30 and (GT)15-SWCNTs in response to acetylthiocholine, which was due to the hydrolysis of the acetyl group and alteration of the DNA corona structure [174]. To establish the selectivity of the CNT probe, the authors carried out competitive experiments with other thiol-containing biological analytes, such as cysteine, but observed only a very insignificant enhancement of fluorescence [175].

Thus, CNT-based biosensors upholds a great deal of advantages in terms of prompt identification of fatal diseases like diabetes, cancer, and other neurodegenerative diseases [176]. Although CNTs can act as efficient diagnostic tools for biologically relevant analytes, their usage is limited due to their poor solubility and strong aggregation nature. To circumvent issues like these, various functional modifications on CNTs are being carried out and newer derivatives of CNTs are only a few years away [177].

Graphene-based, two-dimensional materials are being widely utilized as biosensors for safe and highly sensitive diagnosis of cardiac problems and different types of cancer biomarkers [178]. The 2D structure of graphene provides a flexible and open structure, small band-gap, large surface-to-volume ratio, easy structural modification and great electrical conductivity [179]. All these physicochemical properties enable graphene-based materials to formulate biosensor devices.

Following Covid-19, remote or mobile monitoring and treatment of diseases have been attracting utmost attention for minimizing human contact. In this regard, researchers have taken much interest in the development of wearable biosensing devices. Following this approach, Ozturk et al. constructed a wearable biosensing device from graphene-textiles [179]. In their work, a graphene oxide-based left-arm ECG device was fabricated on a textile material with graphene oxide by dip-coating and spray-printing methods. The as-prepared, single-arm ECG device had successfully monitored the real-time heart rate, R-R interval, and heart rate variability with great precision and higher signal-to-noise ratio than other electrodes like Ag/AgCl for research participants with an average age of 31 years. Further, the authors monitored the daily wearability of this graphene-textile device [179]. This device has been proved to enjoy high wearability owing to its low cost, small size, and compact structure compared to other commercially available, wearable textile-based ECG devices like LevMed ECG Strap, Qardio and Vivalink [180].

Lin et al. devised a biointerface composed of a three-layered structure of graphene/polymethyl methacrylate/copper foil for the smooth diagnosis of cardiac arrhythmias, thereby avoiding expensive implants like pacemakers [181]. A graphene electronic tattoo was constructed by exploiting the excellent stretchability, high strength (130 GPa), optical transparency, and small thickness in the nanometer scale of the graphene materials. The cardiac electrophysiology was accurately monitored using this electrode, which led to early diagnosis of cardiac malfunction [182]. Besides analyzing cardiac functions, various graphene derivatives have been used to selectively detect biomarkers like RNA, enzymes, proteins, etc., whose irregularity can cause serious diseases like cancer. In a recent work, Deng et al. formulated a solution-gated graphene-based transistor for the detection and quantification of miRNA associated with prostate cancer [183]. The biointerface was contrived by depositing graphene film on an Au electrode, which was deposited on a glass substrate. Finally, a single strand DNA was functionalized on the surface of the graphene-modified Au electrode. Here, the DNA acted as the probe to selectively recognize RNA strands and shift the Dirac point voltage to a more positive value [184]. The shift in Dirac voltage occurred within 30 minutes of the interaction between DNA and RNA strands; however, after 1 hour, no significant change in voltage was noticed. This observation validated the fast response time for this graphene-based biointerface. The detection limit was calculated as 10–15 attomoles (aM) [185].

Apart from its use as electrochemical biosensors, graphene materials are also used for creating promising optical biosensors owing to their fascinating emission properties. One such example comes from Kadhim et al. who synthesized DNA-functionalized graphene oxides for the early detection of lung cancer [186]. In their study, the authors had adsorbed a single strand DNA on the graphene oxide surface, leading to quenching of fluorescence from the strong van der Waals interaction and hydrogen bonds between the hexagonal rings of graphene and the 6-membered nucleobase backbones [187]. When treated with regular healthy DNA, these DNA-graphene conjugates displayed prominent increase in their fluorescence spectra owing to the formation and detachment of double stranded DNA from the graphene oxide surface [188]. However, upon contact with the mutated DNA, graphene oxide conjugates did not show any prominent change in fluorescence spectra. Omission of mutated codons from the EGFR gene is one of the distinct biomarkers for lung cancer patients [189]. Thus, the DNA-graphene oxide conjugates could specifically detect early stages of lung cancer by confirming the absence of any mutated gene. Li et al. presented a viscosity probe based on graphene quantum dots [190]. In their study, a graphene quantum dot and rhodamine conjugate were prepared via π-stacking interaction. The rhodamine derivative was designed with twisted intramolecular charge transfer (TICT), responsible for viscosity-sensitivity. They observed a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process from the graphene quantum dot to the rotation-restricted conformation of rhodamine-derivative [191]. However, under a less viscous medium, the rhodamine derivative had an absorption maximum below 500 nm, which did not fall within the emission range of graphene quantum dots for the FRET process. With gradual increase in viscosity of the medium, emission at 530 nm decreases with a concomitant increase in the 635-nm emission wavelength [192]. Stacking the rhodamine derivative on to graphene quantum oxide played the pivotal role here to bring about effective FRET owing to proximity between the rhodamine derivative and graphene quantum dots. Various diseased cells possess highly viscous intracellular medium like diabetes and cancer. Hence, this graphene quantum dot composite can be greatly beneficial for specific diagnosis of such diseases [193]. There are several other types of graphene-based nanomaterials that have been explored over the years for biosensing and faster disease diagnosis. Further, they can also be used in developing new-age contrast agents.

Carbon dots have found prominence in the field of optical sensors owing to their extraordinary photoluminescence properties compared to other carbon nanomaterials. The fluorescence characteristics of carbon dot particles can be regulated by changing the carbon-containing precursor during their synthesis and substitution by heteroatoms [194–196]. Another important feature of carbon dot is that they can absorb light in the longer visible range to NIR range, which is benign for the physiological system. Further, carbon dots can undergo easy functionalization and have a large number of reaction sites on their surface, making the identification of analytes more facile. Li et al. recently reported a carbon dot-based optical biosensor for Vitamin B9 [197]. Specific detection of folic acid is necessary for early diagnosis of heart attacks or strokes. The mechanism of optical sensing of folic acid or Vitamin B9 in this work was explained in terms of an inner filter effect. Strong overlap between the absorption spectra of folic acid and the excitation spectra of carbon dots indicated a possibility of inner filter effect leading to the quenching of fluorescence of the carbon dots upon successive rise in folic acid content. The detection limit was also found to be 0.28 µM, which was lower than many other folic acid detectors [197]. Owing to their stable photoluminescence properties (stable over 6 months or more) and easy internalization into tissues, carbon dots have been a great choice for bioimaging. There has been development in metal-doped carbon dots, which has been shown to improve the luminescence properties of carbon dots [198]. For example, Tian et al. developed a Ni-doped carbon dot conjugate, which exhibited excellent photoacoustic, magnetic resonance, and photothermal imaging of HeLa cells in the NIR-II window [199]. The doping mechanism was explained in terms of conjugation of Ni with the surface amine functional groups of carbon dots resulting in a bathochromic shift in the UV spectra from 328 to 344 nm. However, due to the strong d-d transition in the metal structure, these doped carbon dots could also absorb light in the longer NIR-II range beyond 1000 nm [200]. This made them an excellent choice for imaging different diseased cells due to the deep penetration and lower energy associated with the NIR-II wavelength. Strong binding between the metal and small carbon dot particles gave them a smooth and fast electron transfer ability, which in turn increased their photothermal conversion efficiency [201]. Hence, such doped carbon dots are useful for photothermal imaging of tumor cells. The high photothermal conversion efficiency of carbon dots are excellent for photoacoustic imaging and photothermal therapy inside cancer cells. Another important finding of this research study was the facile, renal clearance of doped carbon dots owing to their very small size (4.6 nm), which restricts any unwanted post-treatment side effects [201]. Apart from their numerous applications as contrast agents and bioimaging agents, carbon dots have also shown remarkable applications in developing electrochemical biosensors. Recently, Azure A-functionalized carbon dots were prepared for redox-sensitive detection of the amino acid L-glutamate, one of the most important neurotransmitters responsible for the normal functioning of our brains [202,203]. The authors conjugated the azure-loaded carbon dots on a screen-printed carbon electrode through natural polymer chitosan. The cyclic voltammograms (CVs) had shown the presence of two redox couples at −0.45 and −0.63 V, corresponding to the oxidation and reduction of Azure A [203]. Upon addition of the biological reducing agent, NADH, a new peak appeared at −0.15 V in the CV, which confirmed the oxidation of NADH to NAD+. Keeping this in mind, the amount of NADH generated during a biological reaction between glutamate dehydrogenase and NAD+ in the presence of L-glutamate could be monitored and quantified using the above-mentioned carbon dots [203,204].

Exploiting the multi-photoluminescence properties of carbon dots, Zhang et al. recently reported hydrothermal synthesis of carbon dots from 1-naphthylamine having three distinct emission wavelengths in blue, green and orange ranges [202,205]. The multiple fluorescence properties stemmed from the multiple functional groups present on the surface of carbon dots, giving rise to several energy levels. The authors also confirmed the fluorescence peaks to be independent of the excitation wavelength. These carbon dots showed selective detection of acidic pH, metal ion, and uric acid. The pH-sensitivity was confirmed by the bathochromic shift in the emission spectra of an aqueous solution of the carbon dots upon successive addition of sodium chloride [202,206]. This shift in emission spectra was attributed to the protonation of the surface -OH groups on carbon dots. Increase in uric acid quantity in the physiological system may lead to gout. Therefore, selective detection and quantification of uric acid is of utmost importance [202,207]. In the present work, the authors had observed quenching of the blue fluorescent carbon dot by the formation of a coordination complex between the electron-rich functional groups on the surface of carbon dots and the outermost 3d5 orbital of Fe3+. However, upon further addition of uric acid, the fluorescence of carbon dots was restored by converting Fe3+ to Fe2+ [202,208].

In a nutshell, as discussed above, carbon-based nanomaterials have shown enormous utility in sensing several biologically relevant analytes, in bioimaging, or as contrast agents. The main reason behind their widespread use across different biomedical applications include their biocompatibility, stimulating optical properties, structural flexibility, high energy levels and smaller band gap leading to fascinating electrical properties. In addition to the carbon materials discussed above, there are several other materials that have also been utilized in the field of biomedicine, such as fullerenes, carbon nanofibers and carbon nanohorns [209–213]. Recently, Malik and Kostakis reported a fullerene-like microneedle structured like a glassy carbon from varied sources like methane, graphite, or other biocompatible polymers for skin allergic treatment [214]. However, the above discussions and several recent literature indicate the superiority of carbon dots over other carbon nanomaterials owing to their unusually stable photoluminescence properties, water solubility, and presence of multiple functional groups on their surface [215]. On one hand, these varied functional groups induce multiple energy levels prompting diverse energy transfer processes; while on the other hand, these functional groups can be interfering for specific surface functionalization of the carbon dots. Therefore, research on the development of new composites of carbon nanomaterials for enhanced biomedical applications is still ongoing [216].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon nanomaterials in sustainable waste management
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanomaterials, such as graphene and CNTs, offer promising solutions in sustainable waste management. They can be utilized in various ways, including for wastewater treatment, pollutant adsorption, and energy storage. Their high surface area and unique properties make them efficient in removing contaminants from water and air [218]. Additionally, carbon nanomaterials can be synthesized from waste materials, contributing to a circular economy approach [219]. Their application holds potential for mitigating environmental pollution and advancing sustainable waste management practices. Environmental pollution and problems related to waste management is one of the serious issues we are facing in the present scenario [220]. The sewage from industrial and domestic waste may discharge a lot of organic and inorganic pollutants into water bodies as well as into the soil and air. To tackle this dangerous environmental problem, more research is needed, using efficient technologies [221]. Different techniques have so far been employed for the treatment of wastewater, remediation of contaminated sites, and detection of pollutants in the environment [222–227]. Nanomaterials are promising materials that are widely used for environmental applications. Herein, we present a comprehensive analysis of carbon nanomaterials for tackling waste management and environmental pollution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon nanomaterials in remediation of contaminated sites and waste treatment
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanoparticles are versatile materials used in wide varieties of environmental applications including sensors for the determination of pollutants, photocatalysts for the degradation of pollutants for remediation of contaminated sites, and as adsorbents for water treatments [217,228–230]. Among the various carbon nanoparticles, CNTs, graphene, graphene oxide, CDs, etc., and their nanocomposites are being explored for analytical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CNTs for treatment and recycling of wastewater
ce:section.ce:para: The role of CNTs in treating and recycling wastewater is a very interesting area of research. Their exceptional structural properties and high surface area enables CNTs to act as promising adsorbents for various organic compounds as well as inorganic ions from contaminated waters [231,232]. A plethora of research has been conducted on the use of CNTs and modified CNT-based materials in environmental applications, especially in recycling and treating wastewater. Recently, de Azevedo et al. highlighted the adsorption and spectroscopic studies on CNTs for the drug, diclofenac potassium (DP), for the removal of DP from wastewater [232,233]. For these studies, both SWCNTs and MWCNTs were synthesized by a chemical method. The SWCNTs and MWCNTs were further structurally characterized by FT-IR, Raman analysis, and morphologically characterized by SEM before and after their interaction with DP [234]. Batch adsorption study was used to find the adsorbing properties of CNTs towards DP in simulated wastewater. The analytical parameter like pH was optimized to get better results for the adsorption of DP in water samples [235]. Results of Raman analysis and FTIR spectra of the SWCNTs and MWCNTs after adsorption with the DP confirmed the successful interaction of DP on the adsorbent [236]. The adsorption of DP with CNTs occurred through the aromatic π-π interaction as well as through the -OH and CO group of DPs with the CNTs as corroborated by the FTIR and Raman results. The thermodynamic and spectroscopic studies were conducted for the adsorption of DP on both SWCNTs and MWCNTs, and proposed a plausible mechanism for the interaction of DP with CNTs (see Fig. 8). Raman studies also showed a strong electron withdrawing tendency by the carbonyl group of DP to the aromatic ring of CNTs [233,237].

A novel nanocomposite of a metal oxide of cobalt and CNTs (Co3O4/CNT-CCM) modified on a ceramic membrane (CCM) was synthesized by Cheng et al. for the recovery and treatment of wastewater [238]. The prepared nanocomposite modified membrane was used for the peroxymonosulfate oxidation of organic pollutants from wastewater and the removal of organic waste during secondary effluent treatment. Peroxymonosulphte oxidation (PMS-oxidation) is a heterogenic catalytic oxidation method, which is widely used in the treatment of water to remove a wide variety of organic pollutants [239]. Studies showed that the carbon surface decorated with cobalt oxide nanoparticles enhanced the catalytic action in the PMS-oxidation/degradation of water pollutants [240]. The synthesized Co3O4/CNT-CCM was characterized by FT-IR, SEM, AFM etc. Also, Co3O4/CNT nanocomposite can be synthesized as the CNTs undergo easy agglomeration in aqueous solutions of cobalt oxides due to the weak secondary bond and hydrophobic interactions of CNTs [241,242]. To get the best catalytic output for the oxidation process of the toxic dye, methylene blue, the composition of the catalyst (Co3O4/CNT) and the dosage of the oxidant on the ceramic membrane was varied. The results showed that the rate of removal of methylene blue was higher on the Co3O4/CNT-CCM than CNT-CCM, Co3O4-CCM and CCM. The synergistic effect of the metal oxide and CNT particles played a catalytic role in the removal of organic pollutant from the ceramic membrane [243]. Co3O4/CNT-CCM was also used for the elimination of dissolved organic carbon from secondary effluents during water treatment. The modified CCM was found to be highly effective in removing organic contents even fluorescent compounds compared to the unmodified CCM, CNT-CCM, and Co3O4-CCM. The Co3O4/CNT-CCM had generated some oxidant species, which powerfully catalyzed the PMS-oxidation of organic contents and performed better in the filtration of secondary effluent treatment of water without any antifouling effect [244].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Safety and regulatory considerations of carbon nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: The safety and regulatory considerations surrounding carbon nanomaterials are paramount due to their expanding range of applications. Concerns primarily revolve around their potential health and environmental impacts [245]. CNTs and graphene, for instance, may pose inhalation risks in occupational settings, akin to asbestos fibers, necessitating stringent workplace controls. Additionally, their fate and behavior in ecosystems are not yet fully understood, demanding ecotoxicity assessments [246]. To address these concerns, regulatory agencies like the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and the European Chemicals Agency (ECHA) have initiated regulatory frameworks and guidelines [247]. Manufacturers are often required to submit toxicity and exposure data for risk assessment. Moreover, labeling and safety data sheets are obligatory to inform users about proper handling and protective measures [248]. Comprehensive research and collaboration between scientists, regulators, and the industry are vital to ensure the safe development and applications of carbon nanomaterials while minimizing potential hazards [249].

The toxicity and biocompatibility of carbon nanomaterials are essential considerations in their diverse applications. CNTs, graphene, and fullerenes exhibit unique physicochemical properties that raise concerns about their potential health effects [250]. Inhalation exposure to certain carbon nanomaterials can lead to respiratory inflammation and fibrosis, similar to asbestos-related diseases. Moreover, their interaction with biological systems depends on size, surface functionalization, and route of exposure [251]. While some studies suggest adverse effects on cell viability and DNA damage, others reveal potential therapeutic applications in drug delivery, imaging, and tissue engineering. Achieving biocompatibility often involves surface modification to enhance cellular uptake and reduce toxicity [252]. Regulatory bodies such as the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) and the European Medicines Agency require rigorous safety assessments for medical and pharmaceutical applications [253]. In summary, understanding the complex interplay between carbon nanomaterials and living systems is crucial for harnessing their potential benefits while mitigating potential risks [254]. Nanomaterials, due to their unique properties, pose potential risks to human health and the environment. Their small size and high surface area can lead to increased reactivity and toxicity [255]. Nanoparticle uptake by cells can lead to lung inflammation, oxidative stress, and other adverse effects. Skin contact or ingestion of certain nanomaterials may also pose health risks. Moreover, concerns exist regarding their environmental impact [256]. Nanoparticles can enter ecosystems through various routes, potentially affecting aquatic and terrestrial organisms. Their long-term behavior, bioaccumulation potential, and ecological consequences are still not fully understood. Furthermore, some nanomaterials may persist in the environment and accumulate over time, raising concerns about their broader ecological impact [257]. Regulatory bodies worldwide are working to establish guidelines and safety assessments to mitigate these risks, but ongoing research and vigilance are necessary to ensure the responsible development and use of nanomaterials [258].

Strategies for evaluating and mitigating the risks associated with nanomaterials involve a multifaceted approach. Firstly, comprehensive toxicity assessments should be conducted, considering the specific nanomaterial properties (size, shape, surface chemistry) and exposure routes [259]. In vitro and in vivo studies can help to identify potential health hazards, while environmental fate studies can gauge ecological risks. Mitigation strategies include engineering nanomaterials with reduced toxicity through surface modifications or encapsulation [260]. Additionally, adopting strict workplace controls, like ventilation and personal protective equipment, is vital to minimize occupational exposure. Developing safe handling protocols and guidelines for nanomaterial production and use is crucial [261]. Furthermore, robust regulations are necessary to govern nanomaterials' production, labeling, and disposal. Manufacturers should provide clear safety data sheets to inform users about handling precautions. Lastly, fostering public awareness and education about nanomaterial risks and benefits are essential [262]. Encouraging responsible research, collaboration among scientists, regulators, and industries, and continuous monitoring of nanomaterials' environmental and health impacts are key elements in ensuring their safe development and use [263].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Regulation and guidelines of carbon nanomaterials toxicity
ce:section.ce:para: Regulating the toxicity of carbon nanomaterials is a critical aspect of ensuring their safe use. Various regulatory bodies worldwide, such as the U.S. EPA, the ECHA, and the U.S. FDA, have initiated guidelines and frameworks to address the potential risks associated with carbon nanomaterials [264]. These regulations often require manufacturers to conduct thorough toxicity assessments and provide safety data sheets. Additionally, labeling and proper handling guidelines are obligatory to inform users about potential hazards [264]. In medical and pharmaceutical applications, rigorous safety assessments are mandatory, and nanomaterial-based products are subjected to the same regulatory scrutiny as conventional products [265]. Environmental regulations may vary by region but typically require risk assessments and environmental impact evaluations. Ongoing research and collaboration between regulatory bodies, scientists, and industry stakeholders are essential to continuously refine and adopt regulations to the evolving understanding of carbon nanomaterial toxicity [266].

Standardized testing methods and risk assessment protocols play a pivotal role in ensuring the safety and reliability of products, processes, and technologies across various industries. They provide a systematic and consistent framework for evaluating potential risks and hazards associated with new materials, chemicals, pharmaceuticals, or technologies, fostering confidence among consumers, regulators, and stakeholders [267]. By adhering to established testing standards, researchers and manufacturers can generate robust, reproducible data that facilitates objective decision-making and risk management. These standardized methods could help to identify and quantify potential adverse effects, such as toxicity, environmental impact, or product failure, allowing for informed risk mitigation strategies and regulatory compliance [268]. Moreover, they promote transparency and accountability, as stakeholders can review and verify the testing procedures and results. In essence, standardized testing and risk assessment protocols serve as the cornerstone of product safety, environmental protection, and public health, facilitating innovation while minimizing potential harm. Their importance is particularly pronounced in industries where human health and the environment are at stake, fostering responsible research, development, and commercialization practices while safeguarding society from unforeseen risks [269].
