ce:section.ce:section-title: Future perspectives and challenges of carbon nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: The future of carbon nanomaterials holds immense promise across diverse fields, including electronics, medicine, energy, and materials science. Carbon nanotubes and graphene, among others, exhibit extraordinary properties such as high conductivity, mechanical strength, and biocompatibility, opening doors to revolutionary applications [270]. In electronics, they could enable faster, smaller, and more efficient devices. In medicine, carbon nanomaterials may revolutionize drug delivery, diagnostics, and tissue engineering. Energy storage and conversion technologies stand to benefit from their exceptional properties [271]. However, significant challenges persist, including scalability and cost-effectiveness of production, environmental and health concerns, and regulatory frameworks to ensure safe use. The potential for unintended environmental impacts also necessitates a deeper understanding of nanomaterial behavior in ecosystems [272]. Balancing innovation with responsible development and addressing these challenges will be paramount to harnessing the full potential of carbon nanomaterials in a sustainable and beneficial manner. Research, collaboration, and regulatory vigilance will shape the future landscape of these transformative materials [273].

Emerging nanomaterials represent a cutting-edge frontier in materials science, offering remarkable properties and transformative potential. Two key categories include 2D materials, exemplified by graphene, with applications in electronics, sensors, and advanced coatings due to their exceptional conductivity and mechanical strength [274]. Quantum dots, on the other hand, are semiconductor nanocrystals with unique optical properties, revolutionizing displays, imaging, and quantum computing [275]. Carbon nanotubes and nanowires promise breakthroughs in nanoelectronics and composite materials. Beyond these, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) exhibit exceptional porosity and tuneable properties, with applications in gas storage, catalysis, and drug delivery [276]. As these nanomaterials further advance, interdisciplinary research and responsible development are vital to harness their full potential while addressing environmental, safety, and ethical considerations. The latest advancements in nanomaterial synthesis have seen the emergence of innovative techniques such as bottom-up assembly, where nanoparticles are precisely arranged to create complex structures with tailored properties [277]. Additive manufacturing, or 3D printing, has extended to nanoscale, enabling the fabrication of intricate nanomaterial-based devices. Furthermore, continuous-flow microreactors have gained prominence for their ability to produce nanoparticles with precise control over size and morphology [278]. In terms of characterization, advanced imaging methods like cryo-electron microscopy and high-resolution atomic force microscopy provide unprecedented insights into nanomaterial structures at the atomic and molecular levels. Spectroscopic techniques, including Raman and infrared spectroscopy, facilitated the analysis of chemical compositions and electronic properties, while machine learning algorithms are enhancing data analysis and pattern recognition for more accurate characterization of nanomaterials [279]. These advancements are driving the progress in nanotechnology applications across various fields, from electronics to healthcare.

Scalability and manufacturing of carbon nanomaterials, such as carbon nanotubes and graphene, are pivotal for their widespread applications. While laboratory-based methods have yielded remarkable properties, transitioning to large-scale production remains challenging [280]. Innovative techniques, including chemical vapor deposition and liquid-phase exfoliation, are advancing scalability. However, ensuring consistent quality, controlling impurities, and optimizing cost-effectiveness are ongoing concerns [281]. Achieving economies of scale is vital to make carbon nanomaterials more accessible for industries like electronics, energy storage, and composites. Collaborative efforts between researchers, manufacturers, and regulatory bodies are essential to drive progress, uphold safety standards, and realize the transformative potential of these materials [282]. Ethical considerations surrounding carbon nanomaterials encompass several key aspects. Firstly, safety and potential health risks must be rigorously assessed to protect workers, consumers, and the environment [283]. Transparency in disclosing the use of nanomaterials in consumer products is also essential for informed decision-making. Intellectual property and fair distribution of benefits from nanomaterial innovations must be addressed. Moreover, there are ethical concerns related to the potential misuse of nanotechnology, including surveillance applications or unintended environmental consequences [284]. Ensuring equitable access to nanotechnology benefits while minimizing harm, promoting responsible research and innovation, and addressing societal concerns through open dialogue and regulations are crucial for navigating the ethical landscape of carbon nanomaterials [285].

In summary, the application of carbon nanomaterials in the fields of biomedical and waste management represents a remarkable convergence of cutting-edge science and pressing societal needs. These advanced materials, including carbon nanospheres, CNTs, graphene, and CQDs, offer a diverse toolkit for addressing critical challenges. The fabrication and types of carbon nanomaterials are as diverse as their applications. Carbon nanospheres, with their spherical structure and tunable properties, hold promise in targeted drug delivery and medical imaging, improving the precision and efficacy of therapeutic interventions. CNTs, with their remarkable mechanical strength and electrical conductivity, find utility in various biomedical applications, including drug delivery systems, biosensors, and tissue engineering. Graphene, renowned for its two-dimensional structure and biocompatibility, stands at the forefront of innovative solutions in drug delivery, tissue regeneration, and biosensing. CQDs, with their quantum confinement effects, present opportunities in bioimaging, biosensing, and enhanced diagnostic capabilities. This versatility in carbon nanomaterial types and fabrication methods paves the way for tailored solutions to complex challenges in healthcare and environmental management.

Due to the peculiar optical, electronic and morphological properties, carbon nanomaterials and their hybrid materials are used in numerous environmental applications. Their porous nature along with higher surface area provides numerous adsorption sites (as adsorbents) for water treatments from contaminated water bodies mainly through π bonded interactions and secondary bond forces. New carbon nanocomposite-based membranes are also being developed for wastewater treatment, motivating researchers to explore this area to a wide extent. Also, the photocatalytic properties of carbon nanocomposites unveil the use of carbon nanomaterials in the removal and degradation of toxic chemicals from the environment in an ecofriendly and cost-effective way. Carbon nanomaterial-based electrochemical sensors and fluorescent sensors have contributed much to environmental monitoring and waste management. Despite the amount of research being done using carbon nanomaterials, there is still more room to expand the scope and applications of carbon nanomaterials in environmental applications. This review tries to encourage researchers to exploit the properties of carbon nanomaterials to develop technologies for sustained, innovative solutions to environmental problems. In the realm of biomedical applications, carbon nanomaterials are revolutionizing the healthcare landscape. By enabling precise drug delivery, reducing side effects, and enhancing the capabilities of diagnostic tools, carbon nanomaterials are improving patient outcomes and the quality of life. Additionally, their potential in tissue engineering have opened doors to regenerative medicine, with the prospect of replacing and repairing damaged or diseased tissues. Meanwhile, in waste management, carbon nanomaterials play a pivotal role in addressing environmental concerns. They can efficiently remove contaminants from water and soil, contributing to the preservation of ecosystems and human well-being. Their versatility and sustainability make carbon nanomaterials indispensable tools for the challenges of the 21st century, bridging the gap between scientific innovation and practical solutions.
